coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of stable metal-free CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions with efficiently enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Yuanzhi
Meng, Yadong
Zhang, Guangyi
Yin, Bingxin
Zhao, Yong
Shi, Weidong
Li, Changsheng
coredata.dc:description: Novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/graphitic-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) metal-free heterojunctions were firstly synthesized via a facile low temperature process. Multiple physicochemical techniques were carried out to characterize the samples. The CQDs with the average size of about 5nm are attached to the surfaces of g-C3N4 nanosheets. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples was carefully evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) under visible light irradiation. Photocatalysis result displayed that the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions can significantly enhance visible-light photocatalytic activity in compared to that of pristine g-C3N4. The reaction rate constant of the optimum active C0.50%/CN heterojunction for the photodegradation of RhB and TC-HCl were about 3.7-fold and 1.9-fold higher than that of bare g-C3N4, respectively. The efficiently enhanced photoactivity was attributed to the CQDs not only can improve the photogenerated electron–hole pair’s separation, but also can increase light harvesting by the up-conversion photoluminescence behavior. Moreover, the as-prepared C0.50%/CN sample also exhibited excellent photostability and recyclability for both photodegradation of RhB and TC-HCl. In addition, the possible enhanced photocatalytic mechanism was proposed and analyzed by the active species trapping experiments. This work will bring about potential for application in environmental purification and useful for designing other CQDs-based photocatalyst in energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
g-C3N4
Heterojunctions
Rhodamine B
Tetracycline hydrochloride

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21276116 , 21477050 , 21301076 , 21303074 , 21522603 and 21576121 ), the Chinese-German Cooperation Research Project ( GZ1091 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2015M571689 ), the Excellent Youth Foundation of Jiangsu Scientific Committee ( BK20140011 ), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University ( NCET-13-0835 ), the Jiangsu Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 1402100C ), the Henry Fok Education Foundation ( 141068 ) and Six Talents Peak Project in Jiangsu Province ( XCL-025 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of as-prepared samples: (a) pure g-C3N4; (b) C0.25%/CN; (c) C0.50%/CN; (d) C1.0%/CN; (e) C2.0%/CN.
(a) HRTEM images of as-prepared samples: (a) pure g-C3N4; (b) C0.25%/CN; (c) C0.50%/CN; (d) C1.0%/CN; (e) C2.0%/CN.
(a) DRS of as-prepared samples and (b) Up-converted PL spectra of CQDs. The insert picture displays the digital pictures of as-prepared samples.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared samples for degradation of (a) RhB and (b) TC-HCl under visible light irradiation; The pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics for (c) RhB and (d) TC-HCl degradation; The apparent rate constants for (e) RhB and (f) TC-HCl degradation.
The PL emission spectra (λex =350nm) of as-prepared samples.
The transient photocurrent response of as-prepared samples.
The species trapping experiments for degradation of RhB and TC-HCl over C0.50%/CN samples under visible light irradiation.
The recycled photocatalytic experiments of C0.50%/CN sample for degradation of (a) RhB and (b) TC-HCl under visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the possible photocatalytic processes with enhanced photocatalytic activity in the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions under visible light.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of stable metal-free CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions with efficiently enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/graphitic-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) metal-free heterojunctions were firstly synthesized via a facile low temperature process. Multiple physicochemical techniques were carried out to characterize the samples. The CQDs with the average size of about 5nm are attached to the surfaces of g-C3N4 nanosheets. The photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples was carefully evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) under visible light irradiation. Photocatalysis result displayed that the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions can significantly enhance visible-light photocatalytic activity in compared to that of pristine g-C3N4. The reaction rate constant of the optimum active C0.50%/CN heterojunction for the photodegradation of RhB and TC-HCl were about 3.7-fold and 1.9-fold higher than that of bare g-C3N4, respectively. The efficiently enhanced photoactivity was attributed to the CQDs not only can improve the photogenerated electron–hole pair’s separation, but also can increase light harvesting by the up-conversion photoluminescence behavior. Moreover, the as-prepared C0.50%/CN sample also exhibited excellent photostability and recyclability for both photodegradation of RhB and TC-HCl. In addition, the possible enhanced photocatalytic mechanism was proposed and analyzed by the active species trapping experiments. This work will bring about potential for application in environmental purification and useful for designing other CQDs-based photocatalyst in energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, photocatalysis has been widely investigated and applied because it can utilize the inexhaustible and clean solar light energy for resolving the environmental and energy issues [1–4]. The semiconductor TiO2 is the most commonly used photocatalyst for environmental remediation by removal of organic contaminants in wastewater [5,6]. However, TiO2 can only response to ultraviolet (UV) light because of its band gap larger than 3.0eV. In this respect, development of visible-light photocatalyst with efficient using the solar energy has become the current research focuses in the field of photocatalysis. Recently, g-C3N4 has received extensively attention in photocatalysis due to its some excellent characteristics, such as physical-chemical stability, good optical-electronic properties, and easily large-scale preparation [7–12]. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic efficiency of pure g-C3N4 is still far from satisfactory, which is mainly due to the rapid photogenerated electron-hole recombination and narrow visible-light absorbance [11,13]. Recently, the construction of heterojunction photocatalysts is an efficient and important strategy to overcome the drawback because it not only can promote the photoexcited electron–hole separation, but also can extend the spectral responsive range [14,15]. Up to date, various g-C3N4-based heterojunctions with high photoactivity have been designed for application in organic pollutants degradation, such as AgVO4 [16–18], Bi2WO6 [19–21], BiPO4 [22,23], Bi2O3 [24,25], Cu2O [26], Fe3O4 [27,28], InVO4 [29], SmVO4 [30], TiO2 [31,32], WO3 [33,34], and ZnFe2O4 [35,36]. That is to say, the constructing of g-C3N4-based photocatalysts has received much attention in resolving the increasing serious environmental contamination.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new carbon nanomaterials, has been widely used as an efficient component in design of photocatalyst owing to its excellent characteristics, such as high aqueous solubility, strong size effect, up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) behavior, and good photoinduced electron transfer properties [37,38]. Very recently, many CQDs/semiconductor heterojunctions have been reported for improving their photocatalytic performance, including ZnO [39], CdS [40], TiO2 [37,41,42], Bi2WO6 [43], Ag3PO4 [44], etc. Nevertheless, both TiO2 and ZnO cannot active under the visible-light region, which will significantly limit their in solar energy conversion. On the other hand, CdS is not stable enough and Ag3PO4 is more expensive, which will bring about great difficulties to their practical application. More recently, Kang et al. [45] have reported CQDs decorated with g-C3N4 to enhance the photocatalytic oxidation water splitting. This work actually motivated us with further interest to design the efficient and stable metal-free photocatalyst with highly photocatalytic activity in environmental remediation. To the best of our knowledge, there are few literature has reported the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions as photocatalyst for removal of organic pollutants from wastewater.

In the present study, we have firstly fabricated of the novel CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts through a facile low temperature method. The CQDs with the average size of about 5nm were distributed on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalysts were carefully investigated by the degradation of RhB and TC-HCl under visible light irradiation (λ>420nm), respectively. The as-prepared CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions could significantly enhance visible-light photocatalytic activity in compared with that of pure g-C3N4. Moreover, the as-obtained C0.50%/CN sample shows the optimum photocatalytic activity as well as possesses excellent photocatalytic stability even after five times recycling photocatalytic RhB and TC-HCl degradation. In addition, when CQDs modified with g-C3N4, it could greatly improve the photogenerated charge carriers separation and increase visible-light harvesting capability. Furthermore, the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity was also proposed based on the active species trapping experiments.

Melamine, ethylenediamine, citric acid, 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), iso-propanol (IPA), triethanolamine (TEOA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and oleic acid (OLA) were analytical grade agents and purchased from Aladdin (China). RhB and TC-HCl were analytically pure and used without further purification. The deionized water was used in all experimental process.

The CQDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process according to the previous literature [38]. Typically, 1.0507g citric acid and 335μL ethylenediamine was dissolved in 10mL water. Then the above solution was transferred to a 30mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 5h. After the reaction, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. In order to obtained the CQDs, the product was subjected to dialysis using a semi-permeable membrane.

The g-C3N4 was prepared by heating melamine as the precursor according to reported method in a muffle furnace as described elsewhere [12]. After that, the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions were synthesized by a facile low temperature process, in which g-C3N4 were dispersed in CQDs solution. In a typical procedure, different amount of CQDs was dissolved in 30mL water, and then 200mgg-C3N4 were dispersed in the CQDs solution. After continued string for 12h, the above solution was dried at 80°C overnight. The added contents of CQDs were 0.25wt%, 0.50wt%, 1.0wt%, 2.0wt%, and referred to as C0.25%/CN, C0.50%/CN, C1.0%/CN and C2.0%/CN, respectively.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were carried out to determine the phase and crystal structures by using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54178Å), (D/MAX-2500 diffractometer, Rigaku, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were examined by transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2010, Japan). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were also used to characterization the as-prepared sample by field emission electron microscopy (JEM-2100F, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (DRS) of the as-synthesized samples were obtained from an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan). X-rays photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a VG ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using a nonmonochromatized Al Kα X-ray source. The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of as-prepared samples were measured by a NOVA 3000e (America) nitrogen adsorption apparatus. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of as-prepared samples were measured on a Perkin-Elmer LS 55 at room temperature using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The photocurrent measurements were obtained using a CHI-852C (CH Instruments) electrochemical workstation by a standard three-electrode system.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were carefully investigated by the photocatalytic degradation of two kinds of pollutants (RhB and TC-HCl) under visible light irradiation. The photoreactor was illuminated using a 250W xenon lamp with a 420nm UV-cut filter, which was located with a distance of 10cm at one side of the containing solution. In each test, the photochemical reactor contains 50mg catalysts and 100mL of 10mg/L pollutants aqueous solution. Then the suspension was stirred in the dark for 30min to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium prior to visible light irradiation. Moreover, the condensed water was introduced through the jacket to cool the solution. During irradiation, 5mL of the suspension was taken out and centrifuged to remove the catalyst before measurement. The peak absorbencies of RhB and TC-HCl at 553nm and 357nm were used to determine its concentration by a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. Finally, the photocatalytic degradation rate (DR%) was calculated by the following formula:
(1)
DR % = [ 1 - ( A /
A
0
) ] × 100 %
where A 0 is the UV–vis absorption of the original solution of pollutants that reached absorption equilibrium, and A is the UV–vis absorption of degraded solution at the certain time.

In order to study the main active species in CQDs/g-C3N4 composites system, TEOA (1mM) [32], BQ (1mM) [16,26], and IPA (1mM) [16] were selected as the scavengers for trapping holes (h+), superoxide radical (
O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (
OH), respectively. The experimental processes are similar to the above photocatalytic reaction except to add the above scavengers, respectively.

The crystalline phases of the as-prepared samples were determined by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks of the pure g-C3N4 appears at 13.1° and 27.4°, which can be indexed to the (100) and (002) diffraction planes of graphite-like carbon nitride. When the CQDs introduced on the surface of g-C3N4, the characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 are gradually become weak. Moreover, no CQDs diffraction peaks are discovered, which may be due to the small amounts, high dispersion and low crystallinity of CQDs in CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions [37]. In addition, there are no other impurities phases are appeared, revealing that the as-prepared photocatalysts are well crystallized.

The morphologies of as-prepared samples were characterized by SEM (Fig. S1), it can be seen that the pure g-C3N4 was consisted of different nanosizes layer-like structures with some smooth surface. After modified with CQDs, the morphologies of CQDs/g-C3N4 samples have no obviously changed. Moreover, the microstructures of as-prepared samples were further determined by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 , TEM image of pure g-C3N4 shows a two-dimensional sheet-like structure with smooth surface. The two-dimensional sheet-like structures also can be observed in the CQDs/g-C3N4 samples, except that appearing some black dots on their surfaces. The HRTEM were further used to demonstrate the existence of CQDs on the surface of g-C3N4. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the lattice fringe of g-C3N4 is hard to be observed, which is similar to our pervious reported [46]. For CQDs/g-C3N4 samples, it can be found that some black dots with an average size of about 5nm are existing, and the clearly lattice spacing of 0.321nm can be corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs [37,44]. Moreover, more and more CQDs can be observed with the increasing the amount of CQDs. Therefore, the results indicate that the CQDs are successfully introduced on the surface of g-C3N4.

Fig.4 a displays the DRS of as-prepared samples. It can be found that the pure g-C3N4 possesses absorption wavelength from the UV to the visible light region which can up to about 460nm. When the CQDs modified with g-C3N4, the absorption edges of the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterostructures (CQDs content from 0.25wt% to 2.0wt%) exhibit a systematic slight red-shift, which illustrates that the CQDs can extend the visible-light spectral responsive range. The phenomenon is agreed with the samples colors change which from light yellow turned to dark brown (see the insert picture in Fig. 4). Moreover, it can be observed that the UV absorption is improved, which is consistent with the pervious literature that low amount of CQDs introducing also can extend the UV absorption [37]. The DRS result suggests that all of the prepared photocatalysts have visible light response and can use as visible-light-driven materials. In addition, the band gap energy (E g) of the photocatalyst can be calculated by the following formula:
(2)
E
g
= 1240 / λ
where λ is the absorption edge of the sample. As shown in the picture, the λ of g-C3N4 was at about 460nm, and the E g of g-C3N4 was estimated to be 2.70eV which is equal to the previous reports [16,34]. To explore CQDs optical property, the up-converted PL spectra were studied using different excitation wavelengths ranging from 600 to 1000nm and are shown in Fig.4b. It can be clearly observed that CQDs can absorb visible light (600–800nm) and near-infrared light (800–1000nm) then emit photoluminescence at a shorter wavelength (400–600nm). Thus, the CQDs can convert visible light and near-infrared light to shorter wavelengths for exciting g-C3N4, leading to g-C3N4 photocatalyst can effectively harness the broad spectrum of sunshine to enhance the photocatalytic performance.

The surface chemical compositions of as-prepared samples were investigated by XPS (Fig. S2). It can be observed that both C and N elements are detected in the survey spectra of all samples. In the high-resolution spectrum, the C1s and N1s peaks of pure g-C3N4 at 284.8eV and 397.9eV can be attributed to the carbon in the form of NCN groups and nitride in the form of CNC groups of graphite-like carbon nitride, respectively. After modified with CQDs, the C1s and N1s peaks of CQDs/g-C3N4 samples exhibited no obvious deviation, suggesting the chemical states of g-C3N4 in composites have no changed. In addition, the BET surface areas of as-prepared samples were obtained by the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S3). The BET surface area of pure g-C3N4 is determined to be 6.898m2/g, indicating its low surface area. Compared to the pristine g-C3N4, the CQDs modified with g-C3N4 could significantly enhance the BET surface area, and the BET data of C0.25%/CN, C0.5%/CN, C1.0%/CN, and C2.0%/CN are determined to be 8.044m2/g, 48.495m2/g, 59.501m2/g, and 54.133m2/g, respectively. The large BET surface area can facilitate catalyst to enhance photocatalytic activity.

RhB is one of the model dye pollutant, which has been widely used to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. Fig.5 a shows the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples for degradation of RhB under visible-light. The blank experiment indicates that the photolysis process of RhB is very slowly which can be negligible. It can be found that the pure g-C3N4 exhibits poor photocatalytic activity (55.8%) in degradation of RhB. However, when CQDs introduced on the surface of g-C3N4, the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts can be significantly enhanced their photocatalytic activities. After irradiated for 210min, 63.5%, 95.2%, 86.8%, and 74.7% of RhB were photodegraded with increasing the CQDs content from 0.25 to 2.0wt%, respectively. In addition, the 0.50wt% CQDs modified C0.50%/CN sample displays the highest RhB degradation rate. On the other hand, TC-HCl is a famous broad-spectrum antibiotic, which has been widely used as the model photodegradation reactions for eliminating the indirect dye photosensitization [47,48]. Fig.5b depicts the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared samples for degradation of TC-HCl under visible-light. It can be observed that the photolysis test reveals that the colorless TC-HCl molecules are very stable and can difficult to be degraded without photocatalyst. Moreover, it also can be found that the pure g-C3N4 exhibits lower activity (54.6%) in TC-HCl degradation. On the contrary, the as-prepared CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions as well as can improve photocatalytic performance, which is similar to the above RhB degradation. After irradiation for 240min, 59.5%, 78.6%, 70.9%, and 63.1% of TC-HCl were photodegraded by using 0.25wt% CQDs/g-C3N4, 0.50wt% CQDs/g-C3N4, 1.0wt% CQDs/g-C3N4 and 2.0wt% CQDs/g-C3N4 composites, respectively. In addition, the 0.50wt% CQDs modified C0.50%/CN sample as well as exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity in TC-HCl degradation. Therefore, these results suggest that the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts not only can efficiently degrade RhB, but also can decompose colorless TC-HCl under visible-light, which can be a good candidate for application in environmental purification.

In order to understand the reactive kinetic behaviors of the pollutants degradation in the CQDs/g-C3N4 system, the RhB and TC-HCl degradation data are further fitted by the pseudo-first-order kinetic plot. Firstly, for RhB, as illustrated in Fig.5c, all of them fit well with the pseudo-first order correlation, suggesting that the RhB degradation photoreactive kinetic is belong to the pseudo-first-order model. Moreover, the reaction rate constant (k) values of different samples are shown in Fig.5e, it can be seen that when the CQDs content is exceeds 0.50wt%, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/g-C3N4 composites decreased as the increasing of CQDs content, and the C0.50%/CN sample exhibits the optimum photocatalytic efficiency which is according to its photocatalytic activity. The k value for RhB degradation over C0.50%/CN sample (0.01438min−1) is about 3.7-fold higher than that of pure g-C3N4 (0.00385min−1). On the other hand, Fig.5d also displays the kinetic behaviors of as-prepared samples for photodegradation of TC-HCl. It can be seen that the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetic model fitted the experimental data are also very well with the pseudo-first order correlation. In addition, Fig.5f shows the k values of different samples, and it can be clearly observed that all of the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions exhibit higher photocatalytic efficiency than that of pure g-C3N4. The optimal loading amount of CQDs on g-C3N4 nanosheet is 0.50wt% according to its photocatalytic activity, which is similar to the result of RhB degradation. The k value for TC-HCl degradation over C0.50%/CN sample (0.00642min−1) is about 1.9-fold higher than that of pure g-C3N4 (0.00333min−1). Thus, the as-prepared CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions could significantly enhance visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation.

The PL analysis usually can be used to estimate the photoinduced charge carriers separation and transfer ability of the as-prepared photocatalysts [16,31]. Before the measurement, the samples (10mg) were well-dispersed 5mL of ethanol, and the PL emission spectra of the samples monitored at an excitation wavelength of 350nm. Fig. 6 depicts the samples’ PL emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 350nm. It can be seen that pure g-C3N4 exhibits the main emission peak at about 455nm and displays the highest PL intensity in compared with that of CQDs/g-C3N4 samples. Compared to pure g-C3N4, the CQDs/g-C3N4 samples show lower PL intensities when CQDs modified with g-C3N4 to form heterojunction, suggesting the recombination rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs become lower. In addition, it can be found that the C0.50%/CN sample possesses the lowest PL intensity, indicating its higher electron–hole pair’s separation and demonstrating its best photocatalytic activity.

The transient photocurrent response as well as an effective tool to evaluate the photogenerated electrons separation and migration of the catalyst [26,43]. Before the test, the photocatalysts were deposited on fluorine-tin oxide (FTO) glass according to the previous reported [49]. Briefly, 0.3g samples were dissolved by 3mL ethanol in a 5mL centrifuge tube, then 0.01g of PVP and 0.03mL of OLA were added in the above solution. After stirred for 2h, the solution was dispersed on the FTO glass, following by a spinning process by a spin coater (Model KW-4A). At last, the FTO glass was dried in vacuum at 80°C for 12h, and the transient photocurrent responses of the samples are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be clearly observed that the C0.50%/CN sample exhibits the highest photocurrent intensity in comparison with that of pure g-C3N4 and others CQDs/g-C3N4 samples. It is well acknowledged that the stronger photocurrent intensity reveals the higher electrons and holes separation efficiency. In other words, the C0.50%/CN sample as well as possesses higher electron–hole pair’s separation ability, suggesting its best photocatalytic performance. Thus, when CQDs modified with g-C3N4, it could greatly improve the photogenerated charge carriers separation and transfer ability, which leading to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 8 depicts the different active species trapping experiments for RhB and TC-HCl degradation over C0.50%/CN sample under visible light irradiation. For RhB, it can be seen that when TEOA (23.7%) and BQ (61.1%) were added, the photodegradation activities are almost inhibited, indicating that h+ and
O2 − play important roles in RhB degradation. On the contrary, after IPA (92.3%) was added, the photodegradation activity is slightly decreased, suggesting that
OH plays a minor role in RhB degradation. For TC-HCl, it can be found that when TEOA (41.1%) and BQ (52.3%) were added, the photodegradation activities are also inhibited, indicating that h+ and
O2 − as well as play important roles in TC-HCl degradation. However, after IPA (75.9%) was added, the photodegradation activity is slightly declined, suggesting that
OH also plays a minor role in TC-HCl degradation. Therefore, the result suggests that h+ and
O2 − are the major reactive species for organic pollutants photocatalytic degradation in the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction systems.

The photocatalytic reusability and repeated performance of the as-prepared C0.50%/CN sample was also studied by the circulation photocatalytic degradation of RhB and TC-HCl under visible light irradiation. After each cycling photocatalytic experiment, the photocatalyst was collected, washed and dried for next recycling. From Fig. 9 , it can be observed that the C0.50%/CN sample exhibits the same photocatalytic degradation activity even after 5th successive recycles for the degradation of RhB (Fig.9a) and TC-HCl (Fig.9b) under visible-light, which suggests that our photocatalyst possess high stability for its practical application.

The possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity over CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions for pollutants under visible light irradiation was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 10 . The electrons and holes were generated and transferred by two different paths: (1) g-C3N4 can be excited under visible-light to yield photogenerated electron–hole pairs and electrons will transfer to the CQDs, then further adsorb O2 to form
O2 − radical. The powerful oxidative specie of
O2 − radical could oxidize organic pollutants into small molecules, e.g., CO2 and H2O. Meanwhile, the photogenerated h+ left behind in the valence band of g-C3N4 can also directly oxidize organic pollutants. (2) The CQDs can absorb light with wavelength longer than 600nm and then convert them to shorter wavelengths owing to the up-converted PL behavior. The shorter wavelengths that less than 460nm also can excite the g-C3N4 to produce electron–hole pairs, leading to further enhancement of photocatalytic activity. Moreover, it should be pointed out that the up-conversion PL effect of the composite may very weak in the photocatalytic process due to the low amount of CQDs. In such ways, the CQDs not only can improve the photogenerated electron–hole pair’s separation, but also can increase visible-light harvesting ability, in which results in efficiency degradation of organic pollutants under visible light irradiation.

In summary, the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts were successfully synthesized by a simple low temperature method. The photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 could be greatly improved by introducing CQDs on the surface of g-C3N4. The loading content of CQDs plays a key role in determining the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions. The 0.50wt% CQDs modified g-C3N4 displays the highest photocatalytic efficiency under visible light irradiation. In addition, the as-prepared samples exhibited efficient and stable visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance in degradation of organic pollutants, which could be used as a candidate for application in environmental remediation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21276116, 21477050, 21301076, 21303074, 21522603 and 21576121), the Chinese-German Cooperation Research Project (GZ1091), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M571689), the Excellent Youth Foundation of Jiangsu Scientific Committee (BK20140011), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-13-0835), the Jiangsu Postdoctoral Science Foundation (1402100C), the Henry Fok Education Foundation (141068) and Six Talents Peak Project in Jiangsu Province (XCL-025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2016.07.025.
